problem_statement: A school district's offline reading application began reporting erratic synchronization latency after a tablet fleet rollout. Some dashboard values are negative or exceed an hour even though reading progress eventually reaches the server and support reports do not show a comparable rise in lost work.

observations.statement: The dashboard shift starts on 2026-03-09, one day after a local daylight-saving transition and during a four-day rollout that increased the new tablet cohort from 8 percent to 61 percent of sampled sessions.
observations.provenance: retained dashboard series and fleet enrollment history

observations.statement: Among anomalous sessions, reported latency clusters near negative 3,600 seconds and positive 3,600 seconds, with smaller groups near zero and above two hours.
observations.provenance: offline histogram from retained synchronization events

observations.statement: The client event stores queue_started as a local wall-time string without an offset, while the server stores receipt time in UTC; the latency job subtracts the parsed client value from server receipt time.
observations.provenance: event schema, retained sample payloads, and metric-job plan

observations.statement: Server receipt sequence, per-device event sequence, and content versions remain monotonic for 99.8 percent of anomalous sessions, so the negative values do not represent reversed delivery or lost reading progress.

Fleet summaries show 73 devices accepted a wall-clock correction between queueing and receipt, but privacy aggregation removed the event-to-device linkage needed to identify which dashboard samples were corrected.
observations.provenance: retained device-management aggregate and privacy retention policy

observations.statement: Desktop clients use server-issued queue tokens and retain a stable latency distribution over the same network and backend interval, providing a negative control for the shared synchronization service.
observations.provenance: cross-client latency and backend health export

observations.statement: Offline recomputation under a one-hour offset correction collapses either the positive or negative cluster depending on the assumed parsing zone, but neither assumption explains the entire long tail.

The pre-rollout latency baseline contains mostly desktop sessions and retained only daily percentiles, not raw queue spans; it cannot establish the tablet cohort's prior actual network-delay distribution.
observations.provenance: metric-retention inventory and cohort composition report

observations.statement: The retained events contain neither a monotonic duration, a UTC offset, nor synchronization state at queue time, so the current archive cannot separate wall-time interpretation error from a simultaneous change in actual offline duration across the new cohort.
